title: Check User Privileges (CMD)
description: Lists the Windows security privileges assigned to the current user token. This is one of the most critical first steps in Windows enumeration.
when_to_use: Run this immediately upon getting a shell. Look specifically for SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege, or SeBackupPrivilege, which are common pathways to full SYSTEM compromise.
common_pitfalls: Just because a privilege is listed does not mean it is currently 'Enabled'. However, an attacker can usually enable a listed privilege programmatically.

title: System Information & Hotfixes (CMD)
description: Extracts the core operating system details from the noisy output of 'systeminfo'. You can also search for applied hotfixes to identify missing kernel patches.
when_to_use: Use this to determine the exact OS build number. You can then cross-reference this build with local exploit suggesters (like Windows-Exploit-Suggester) to find kernel exploits.

title: Get OS Architecture & Details (PowerShell)
command: Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem | Select-Object Caption, Version, OSArchitecture
description: The modern PowerShell equivalent for querying OS details using CIM instances, returning a clean, parseable object.
when_to_use: Preferred over CMD's systeminfo when you have a stable PowerShell session and need precise object-oriented output without string parsing.
example: Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem | Select-Object Caption, Version, OSArchitecture

title: List Local Users and Administrators
description: Displays all local user accounts on the machine, and then specifically lists the members of the powerful local 'Administrators' group.
when_to_use: Crucial for understanding who has access to the machine. If your current user is in the Administrators group, you already have full control (but may need to bypass UAC).

title: Check Folder Permissions (ACLs)
description: Retrieves the Access Control List (ACL) for a specific file or folder, detailing exactly which users or groups have Read, Write, or Execute permissions.
when_to_use: Use this to check if your low-privileged user has write access to sensitive directories, such as 'C:\Program Files\', which is a primary requirement for DLL Hijacking or unquoted service path exploits.
common_pitfalls: PowerShell's native Get-Acl output can be difficult to interpret quickly. Tools like accesschk.exe are often preferred for readability if available.

title: Run Full winPEAS Scan
description: Executes the Windows Privilege Escalation Awesome Scripts suite. It exhaustively searches the host for misconfigurations, hardcoded credentials, vulnerable services, and unpatched exploits.
when_to_use: Run this after manual enumeration if nothing obvious stands out. It automates hundreds of checks in seconds.
common_pitfalls: The output is extremely verbose and color-coded. If you redirect to a text file, the ANSI color codes will make it unreadable unless you use a compatible viewer or strip them.

title: Query Unquoted Service Paths
command: wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "C:\Windows\\" | findstr /i /v """
description: Searches for services that start automatically, are located outside the secure Windows folder, and do NOT have quotes around their executable paths. This is the classic signature of an Unquoted Service Path vulnerability.
when_to_use: A staple manual check. If an unquoted path contains a space (e.g., C:\Program Files\App\run.exe), Windows might try to execute 'C:\Program.exe' first. If you can write to the C:\ drive, you can drop a malicious 'Program.exe' to intercept the service launch.
example: wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "C:\Windows\\" | findstr /i /v """
